channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Resuming War Is a Short-Term Political Boon for Netanyahu, but Grim Choices Await
item.description: If President Trump forces Israel to stand down against Iran, analysts say, it could leave Israel hindered from responding forcefully to attacks by Hezbollah.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Ronen Zvulun
item.media:description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu of Israel in Jerusalem, in March.

item.title: Oil Jumps After Hostilities Between Iran and Israel Escalate
item.description: Oil prices jumped and stocks plunged after Iran and Israel exchanged strikes, raising doubts about the future of fragile cease-fire in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kim Hong-Ji/Reuters

item.title: Iran and Israel Exchange First Strikes Since Cease-Fire, and Texas Ranchers Sound the Alarm
item.description: Plus, the fight over the fight on the White House lawn.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas and Ian Stewart
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Shir Torem/Reuters
item.media:description: Iran and Israel traded waves of attacks on Monday as an uneasy two-month truce that had suspended the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran appeared to have broken down.

item.title: How the Drive to Find a Conspiracy Against Trump Rocked the Justice Dept.
item.description: The push to investigate what the president’s allies saw as a “deep state” cabal intent on taking him down set off cascading crises, ended careers and undercut the department’s credibility with judges.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: The F.B.I. director Kash Patel’s “grand conspiracy case” sought to tie together actions by a group of people that President Trump blamed for various investigations into him.

item.title: Trump Defends Compensation Fund and Iran War in ‘Meet the Press’ Interview
item.description: In a lengthy interview with NBC’s “Meet the Press,” the president again vowed that gas prices would go down when the war in Iran ends.
item.dc:creator: Katie Rogers
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump speaking to reporters on Air Force One on Friday.

item.title: Europe Watches Its Economic Recovery Fade Into the Distance
item.description: As the war in Iran persists, signs point to a prolonged period of higher prices and slower growth rather than a quick shock.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development
item.media:credit: Violette Franchi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A supermarket in Paris. For Europe, the economic drag is now forecast to last into next year.

item.title: Nithya Raman Overtakes Spencer Pratt in Race for L.A. Mayor
item.description: Nithya Raman pulled into second place in the race to see who will face Mayor Karen Bass in November. There are more votes to be counted.
item.dc:creator: Shawn Hubler
item.media:credit: Gabriela Bhaskar/The New York Times
item.media:description: Nithya Raman campaigns in downtown Los Angeles ahead of the mayoral primary last month.

item.title: They Got Platner’s Endorsement for Maine Governor. What Did They Do With It?
item.description: The crowded race for governor has barely qualified as background noise in recent months, drowned out by the high-stakes, turbulent campaign for U.S. Senate.
item.dc:creator: Jenna Russell
item.media:credit: Amanda Sabga/Reuters; Cliff Owen & Joel Page, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Democratic candidates Troy Jackson, a former president of the State Senate; Shenna Bellows, the secretary of state, and Hannah Pingree, a former speaker of the State House.

item.title: In Maine, Supporters of Graham Platner Continue to Back His Senate Campaign, With ‘Trepidation’
item.description: Many are sticking by the presumptive Democratic Senate nominee Graham Platner. But some have soured, and others are anxious about how recent revelations could affect a close race.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk, Alicia Anstead and Kathleen Tomaselli
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner’s campaign has drawn large crowds of enthusiastic supporters in Maine, but many Democrats are anxious about his candidacy.

item.title: South Carolina Governor’s Race Tests the Strength of a Trump Endorsement
item.description: The Republican candidates for South Carolina governor went to extraordinary lengths for the president’s support. But his choice of Lt. Gov. Pamela Evette has not quite sealed the deal among voters.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina and Emily Cochrane
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: The candidates for governor in South Carolina went to extraordinary lengths to garner President Trump’s endorsement.

item.title: Best and Worst Moments From the 2026 Tony Awards: Pink, Maya Rudolph, Cole Escola, ‘Cats’
item.description: Pink brought sincerity and punk energy as the host, musical numbers by “Cats,” “Ragtime” and “Chicago” popped, and Maya Rudolph and Cole Escola mined comic gold.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: Pink, center, flanked by Megan Thee Stallion, Neil Patrick Harris and other performers during the opening number at the Tony Awards.

item.title: Tony Award Winners 2026: See the Full List
item.description: The Tony Awards were held at Radio City Music Hall in New York City.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sherman
item.media:credit: Jutharat Pinyodoonyachet for The New York Times
item.media:description: John Lithgow, the star of “Giant,” with his award for best actor in a play.

item.title: A ‘Miraculous Transformation’: How Kim Jong-un Fortified North Korea
item.description: He used the pandemic to ruthlessly tighten his grip on the country. Then he energized its economy by leveraging Russia’s war in Ukraine.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:credit: Athit Perawongmetha/Reuters
item.media:description: Kim Jong-un arriving at the Dong Dang railway station in Vietnam, near the Chinese border, in 2019.

item.title: What To Know as China’s Xi Jinping Heads to North Korea
item.description: As Xi Jinping visits Pyongyang, he faces an emboldened North Korean dictator, whose alliance with Russia has reduced his dependence on China.
item.dc:creator: David Pierson and Choe Sang-Hun
item.category: Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
item.media:credit: Florence Lo/Reuters
item.media:description: Xi Jinping, China’s leader, with North Korea’s leader, Kim Jong-un, in Beijing in September. Analysts say China is likely keen to assert its influence over North Korea as Pyongyang has leaned toward Russia.

item.title: Trump, Mamdani and the Knicks: A Manic Monday Awaits New York
item.description: When the Knicks return home for Game 3 of the N.B.A. finals, much of New York City will be watching, including some well-known guests.
item.dc:creator: Jonah E. Bromwich, Dana Rubinstein and William K. Rashbaum
item.media:credit: Shuran Huang for The New York Times

item.title: Young Knicks Fans Rejoice Over Their Team’s Run for the Ages
item.description: New Yorkers under 30 know about the past pain and suffering. But now they have reason to believe.
item.dc:creator: Ellen Yan, Jonah E. Bromwich and Jonah Rosenberg

item.title: The New York Hip-Hop Soundtracking the Knicks’ Run to the N.B.A. Finals
item.description: From old-school stars to young upstarts, the birthplace of hip-hop is basking in the big moment.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.media:credit: Jamie Squire/Getty Images
item.media:description: The Rapper Fat Joe has been one of the most visible supporters of the Knicks on their playoff run.

item.title: The American Family Is at a Breaking Point. Our Politics Have Finally Noticed.
item.description: Society has treated parenting as a private endeavor. But what if raising children is also a public good?
item.dc:creator: Jia Lynn Yang

item.title: A.I. Degree Programs Surge as Colleges Seek Students and Relevance
item.description: Colleges from North Dakota to New Jersey are trying to get students to sign up for A.I. degrees. What they teach varies widely.
item.dc:creator: Alan Blinder
item.media:credit: Tim Gruber for The New York Times
item.media:description: University of North Dakota is the flagship school in a state that will soon have two universities offering undergraduate or graduate degrees in A.I.

item.title: Want an A.I. Degree? Here’s What You Should Think About.
item.description: The programs are popping up on campuses across America. What they teach varies.
item.dc:creator: Alan Blinder
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: More universities are offering A.I. degrees, hoping they’ll attract students and make their graduates more marketable.

item.title: Why Your Next Diagnosis May Be Guided by an A.I. Helper
item.description: OpenEvidence, a fast-growing start-up, is using artificial intelligence to help doctors find answers to clinical questions for diagnosis and treatment.
item.dc:creator: Steve Lohr
item.media:credit: Sasha Maslov for The New York Times

item.title: Searching for Shade When It’s 125 Degrees
item.description: Every season brings a new struggle for people in Dadu District, Pakistan, an area prone to sandstorms, drought and flooding.
item.dc:creator: Zia ur-Rehman and Asim Hafeez
item.media:credit: Asim Hafeez for The New York Times

item.title: Apple Expected to Detail Its A.I. Plans at Conference
item.description: For the second time, the company is expected to explain its artificial intelligence plans. Unlike some rivals, it is not reorganizing around the technology.
item.dc:creator: Kalley Huang and Brian X. Chen

item.title: Two New Studies Ask: Did the iPhone Cause Birthrates to Decline?
item.description: Modern smartphones rolled out in 2007, the year that fertility rates began falling. Two studies say that is not a coincidence.
item.dc:creator: Sabrina Tavernise
item.media:credit: Colin Clark for The New York Times
item.media:description: Fertility is not just dropping in the United States. The mystery is now a near-global phenomenon.

item.title: Bruce Springsteen Talks About His Place in American Music
item.description: As he opens an expansive cultural center not far from the Jersey Shore boardwalk, the singer describes himself as “a small link in a big chain.”
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.category: Springsteen, Bruce, Archives and Center for American Music
item.media:credit: Hannah Beier for The New York Times
item.media:description: Bruce Springsteen outside the new Bruce Springsteen Center for American Music.

item.title: Why Everyone Wants Jon Ossoff to Run for President
item.description: The Georgia senator is excoriating Trump and his systemic corruption in a way that transcends the Democratic Party’s progressive-moderate divide.
item.dc:creator: Michelle Goldberg
item.media:credit: José Ibarra Rizo for The New York Times

item.title: What Liberals Get Wrong About the Middle Class
item.description: The middle class is larger than you think, and growing.
item.dc:creator: Stephen J. Rose and Scott Winship

item.title: The Costs of War
item.description: We look at how Israeli trade restrictions and shadowy profiteers have driven up the prices of nearly everything in Gaza.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.media:credit: Gabriella Demczuk for The New York Times
item.media:description: Saleh Abu Shamala, a Palestinian who lives and works in London, sends most of his earnings to his family in Gaza.

item.title: Inside the Subway Expansion a Century in the Making
item.description: The next phase of the Second Avenue subway, first proposed in the 1920s, could finally be completed by 2032, creating a new transit hub in Harlem.
item.dc:creator: Stefanos Chen and Dave Sanders
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
